steps.title: Understanding the constraint
steps.content: For $g(x) > k$ to be true for all values of $x$, the constant $k$ must be at most the greatest lower bound of $g(x)$ — in other words, the smallest value that $g(x)$ approaches. We need to find how small the function can get.

steps.title: Analyzing the exponential component
steps.content:
In $g(x) = \frac{1}{11}(5 \cdot 2^x + 4)$, the behavior depends on $2^x$. The exponential function $2^x$ has these key properties:
  • $2^x > 0$ for all real $x$ (always positive)
  • As $x$ becomes very negative, $2^x \to 0^+$ (approaches 0 from above)
We can find the lower bound of $g(x)$ by examining what happens as $x \to -\infty$.

steps.title: Finding the greatest value of k
steps.content:
As $x \to -\infty$, the term $5 \cdot 2^x$ approaches 0, so:

$$g(x) = \frac{1}{11}(5 \cdot 2^x + 4) \to \frac{1}{11}(5 \cdot 0 + 4) = \frac{4}{11}$$

Since $2^x$ never actually equals zero—it is always strictly positive—the value $5 \cdot 2^x + 4$ is always strictly greater than 4. Therefore, $g(x) > \frac{4}{11}$ for all real $x$. The greatest possible value of $k$ is therefore $\frac{4}{11}$. That matches choice B — B is correct.
